title: Henry Gerber founds the Society for Human Rights in Chicago
description: On 10 December 1924 Henry Gerber established the Society for Human Rights in Chicago, the earliest documented gay rights organization in the United States. It issued Friendship and Freedom, the first American publication aimed at homosexual readers, but folded soon afterward under political and legal pressure.

sources.label: PBS American Experience — Milestones in the American Gay Rights Movement

title: Kinsey publishes Sexual Behavior in the Human Male
description: In 1948 biologist Alfred Kinsey released Sexual Behavior in the Human Male, reporting that same-sex behavior was far more common than assumed and that many men who did not identify as homosexual had engaged in it. The findings challenged prevailing assumptions about sexuality.

sources.label: PBS American Experience — Milestones in the American Gay Rights Movement

title: Harry Hay founds the Mattachine Society in Los Angeles
description: On 11 November 1950 Harry Hay established the Mattachine Society in Los Angeles, one of the earliest sustained gay rights organizations in the United States. It worked to end discrimination against homosexuals and build community support.

sources.label: PBS American Experience — Milestones in the American Gay Rights Movement

title: Senate report frames homosexuals as security risks
description: On 15 December 1950 a Senate report titled Employment of Homosexuals and Other Sex Perverts in Government told Congress that homosexuals posed security risks. The document helped drive the Lavender Scare, during which thousands of military personnel and hundreds of federal employees were forced out of their jobs.

sources.label: PBS American Experience — Milestones in the American Gay Rights Movement

title: APA classifies homosexuality as a mental disorder
description: In April 1952 the American Psychiatric Association listed homosexuality as a sociopathic personality disturbance in the first edition of its Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders, giving official medical weight to the idea that same-sex attraction was a pathology.

sources.label: PBS American Experience — Milestones in the American Gay Rights Movement

title: Executive Order 10450 bars homosexuals from federal jobs
description: On 27 April 1953 President Dwight Eisenhower signed Executive Order 10450, which excluded homosexuals from federal employment and from work with government contractors by classifying them as security risks. The order institutionalized anti-gay discrimination across the federal workforce.

sources.label: PBS American Experience — Milestones in the American Gay Rights Movement

title: Daughters of Bilitis founded in San Francisco
description: On 21 September 1955 the Daughters of Bilitis was established in San Francisco as the first lesbian rights organization in the United States. It offered social gatherings as a safer alternative to bars and clubs, which were frequently raided by police.

sources.label: PBS American Experience — Milestones in the American Gay Rights Movement

title: Evelyn Hooker's research challenges the disease model of homosexuality
description: On 30 August 1956 psychologist Evelyn Hooker presented findings arguing that homosexuality was not a clinical disorder and that homosexual and heterosexual men showed no significant psychological differences. Her work helped shift clinical thinking away from treating homosexuality as an illness.

sources.label: PBS American Experience — Milestones in the American Gay Rights Movement

title: Supreme Court sides with ONE magazine in One, Inc. v. Olesen
description: On 13 January 1958 the U.S. Supreme Court ruled in One, Inc. v. Olesen that ONE: The Homosexual Magazine was protected by the First Amendment, reversing a finding that it was obscene. It was the first Supreme Court decision to favor a homosexual cause.

sources.label: PBS American Experience — Milestones in the American Gay Rights Movement

title: Illinois becomes the first U.S. state to decriminalize homosexuality
description: On 1 January 1962 Illinois became the first U.S. state to repeal its sodomy law, decriminalizing consensual same-sex activity. It stood alone among the states in doing so for nearly a decade.

sources.label: PBS American Experience — Milestones in the American Gay Rights Movement

title: First Annual Reminder pickets at Independence Hall
description: On 4 July 1965 activists held the first Annual Reminder, a picket outside Independence Hall in Philadelphia drawing attention to the lack of civil rights protections for gay and lesbian Americans. The demonstration was repeated each Independence Day for five years.

sources.label: PBS American Experience — Milestones in the American Gay Rights Movement

title: Mattachine Society stages a sip-in at Julius Bar
description: On 21 April 1966 members of the Mattachine Society staged a sip-in at Julius Bar in Greenwich Village to challenge New York rules against serving gay patrons. The action prompted the city's Commission on Human Rights to affirm that homosexuals had the right to be served.

sources.label: PBS American Experience — Milestones in the American Gay Rights Movement

title: Compton's Cafeteria riot in San Francisco
description: In August 1966 transgender patrons and police clashed at a San Francisco cafeteria, sparking a riot in the Tenderloin district. In its wake activists set up the National Transsexual Counseling Unit, described as the first peer-run support and advocacy body of its kind.

sources.label: PBS American Experience — Milestones in the American Gay Rights Movement

title: Stonewall uprising in Greenwich Village
description: On 28 June 1969 patrons of the Stonewall Inn in Greenwich Village resisted a police raid, touching off several days of protests involving thousands of people. The uprising is widely credited with galvanizing the modern LGBTQ rights movement in the United States.

sources.label: PBS American Experience — Milestones in the American Gay Rights Movement

title: Christopher Street Liberation Day, the first gay pride march
description: On 28 June 1970 thousands marked the first anniversary of Stonewall with Christopher Street Liberation Day, marching through New York into Central Park. It is generally regarded as the first gay pride parade and inspired similar events elsewhere.

sources.label: PBS American Experience — Milestones in the American Gay Rights Movement

title: APA removes homosexuality from its list of mental illnesses
description: On 15 December 1973 the American Psychiatric Association voted to remove homosexuality from its official list of mental disorders, reversing the classification it had adopted two decades earlier.

sources.label: PBS American Experience — Milestones in the American Gay Rights Movement

title: Kathy Kozachenko becomes the first openly gay American elected to office
description: In January 1974 Kathy Kozachenko won a seat on the Ann Arbor City Council in Michigan, becoming the first openly gay candidate elected to public office in the United States.

sources.label: PBS American Experience — Milestones in the American Gay Rights Movement

title: Anita Bryant campaign repeals Dade County gay rights ordinance
description: On 7 June 1977 Anita Bryant's Save Our Children campaign succeeded in repealing a gay rights ordinance in Dade County, Florida. The protections were not restored until December 1998.

sources.label: PBS American Experience — Milestones in the American Gay Rights Movement

title: Harvey Milk elected to the San Francisco Board of Supervisors
description: On 8 November 1977 Harvey Milk won a seat on the San Francisco Board of Supervisors, becoming one of the first openly gay elected officials in the country. He championed employment protections for gay people before being assassinated by former supervisor Dan White on 27 November 1978.

sources.label: PBS American Experience — Milestones in the American Gay Rights Movement

title: White Night riots follow the Dan White verdict
description: On 21 May 1979 Dan White received a seven-year sentence for voluntary manslaughter over the killings of Harvey Milk and Mayor George Moscone. Outrage at the lenient verdict drove thousands of protesters to storm San Francisco City Hall, with a larger crowd later gathering on Castro and Market streets.

sources.label: PBS American Experience — Milestones in the American Gay Rights Movement

title: First National March on Washington for Lesbian and Gay Rights
description: On 14 October 1979 an estimated 75,000 people took part in the first National March on Washington for Lesbian and Gay Rights, calling for equal civil rights and federal protective legislation.

sources.label: PBS American Experience — Milestones in the American Gay Rights Movement

title: Democratic Party endorses a gay rights platform
description: On 8 July 1980 the Democratic Rules Committee committed to non-discrimination against homosexuals, and the party went on to endorse a gay rights plank at its August convention, the first major U.S. political party to do so.

sources.label: PBS American Experience — Milestones in the American Gay Rights Movement

title: First press report on what would become AIDS
description: On 3 July 1981 the New York Times published the first report on a rare pneumonia and skin cancer appearing in 41 gay men in New York and California. The Centers for Disease Control initially labeled the condition GRID, or Gay Related Immune Deficiency Disorder.

sources.label: PBS American Experience — Milestones in the American Gay Rights Movement

title: Wisconsin becomes the first state to ban anti-gay discrimination
description: On 2 March 1982 Wisconsin became the first U.S. state to outlaw discrimination on the basis of sexual orientation, establishing legal protections in areas such as employment and housing.

sources.label: PBS American Experience — Milestones in the American Gay Rights Movement

title: ACT UP founded in New York
description: On 10 March 1987 the AIDS Coalition to Unleash Power, known as ACT UP, formed in New York. The group used direct action to protest drug-company profiteering and to demand faster access to treatment and stronger protections for people with AIDS.

sources.label: PBS American Experience — Milestones in the American Gay Rights Movement

title: Second National March on Washington demands AIDS action
description: On 11 October 1987 hundreds of thousands joined a National March on Washington, pressing President Ronald Reagan to confront the AIDS crisis and expand support for those affected.

sources.label: PBS American Experience — Milestones in the American Gay Rights Movement

title: CDC mails Understanding AIDS to every U.S. household
description: Across May and June 1988 the Centers for Disease Control mailed its Understanding AIDS brochure to every household in the United States, sending roughly 107 million copies in the largest public health mailing of its kind.

sources.label: PBS American Experience — Milestones in the American Gay Rights Movement

title: First World AIDS Day
description: On 1 December 1988 the World Health Organization held the first World AIDS Day to raise global awareness of the epidemic and support those living with the disease.

sources.label: PBS American Experience — Milestones in the American Gay Rights Movement

title: Ryan White CARE Act signed into law
description: On 18 August 1990 President George H. W. Bush signed the Ryan White CARE Act, creating a federally funded program to support people living with AIDS. It was named for Ryan White, a teenager infected through tainted hemophilia treatment who became a prominent activist against AIDS discrimination.

sources.label: PBS American Experience — Milestones in the American Gay Rights Movement

title: Red ribbon adopted as a symbol of AIDS awareness
description: In 1991 the artists' group Visual AIDS introduced the red ribbon as a symbol of awareness and solidarity with people living with HIV and AIDS. It became one of the most recognized emblems of the epidemic.

sources.label: PBS American Experience — Milestones in the American Gay Rights Movement

title: Pentagon issues the Don't Ask, Don't Tell directive
description: On 21 December 1993 the Department of Defense issued the Don't Ask, Don't Tell directive. It barred the military from asking about service members' sexual orientation while continuing to prohibit them from being openly gay, a compromise that still forced many to hide their identity.

sources.label: PBS American Experience — Milestones in the American Gay Rights Movement

title: Supreme Court strikes down Colorado's Amendment 2 in Romer v. Evans
description: On 20 May 1996 the U.S. Supreme Court ruled in Romer v. Evans that a Colorado constitutional amendment barring anti-discrimination protections for gay and lesbian people was unconstitutional.

sources.label: PBS American Experience — Milestones in the American Gay Rights Movement

title: Defense of Marriage Act signed into law
description: On 21 September 1996 President Bill Clinton signed the Defense of Marriage Act, which defined marriage in federal law as a union between one man and one woman and freed states from having to recognize same-sex marriages performed elsewhere.

sources.label: PBS American Experience — Milestones in the American Gay Rights Movement

title: Coretta Scott King urges civil rights movement to oppose homophobia
description: On 1 April 1998 Coretta Scott King called on the civil rights community to join the fight against homophobia, drawing a parallel between the struggle for racial equality and the struggle for gay rights that some critics rejected.

sources.label: PBS American Experience — Milestones in the American Gay Rights Movement

title: Vermont becomes the first state to legalize civil unions
description: On 26 April 2000 Vermont became the first U.S. state to legalize civil unions and registered partnerships for same-sex couples, granting them many of the legal benefits of marriage.

sources.label: PBS American Experience — Milestones in the American Gay Rights Movement

title: Supreme Court strikes down sodomy laws in Lawrence v. Texas
description: On 26 June 2003 the U.S. Supreme Court ruled in Lawrence v. Texas that laws criminalizing consensual same-sex conduct were unconstitutional, invalidating remaining sodomy statutes across the country.

sources.label: PBS American Experience — Milestones in the American Gay Rights Movement

title: Massachusetts becomes the first state to legalize same-sex marriage
description: On 18 May 2004 Massachusetts became the first U.S. state to legalize same-sex marriage, after its highest court found that barring gay couples from marrying denied them dignity and equality. Several more states and Washington D.C. followed over the next six years.

sources.label: PBS American Experience — Milestones in the American Gay Rights Movement

title: First U.S. presidential forum on LGBT issues
description: On 9 August 2007 the Logo cable channel hosted the first American presidential forum devoted to LGBT issues. Six Democratic candidates took part, while the Republican candidates declined to attend.

sources.label: PBS American Experience — Milestones in the American Gay Rights Movement

title: California voters pass Proposition 8
description: On 4 November 2008 California voters approved Proposition 8, a ballot measure that banned same-sex marriage in the state. The result energized opposition, including the NOH8 photo campaign for marriage equality.

sources.label: PBS American Experience — Milestones in the American Gay Rights Movement

title: Obama extends some benefits to same-sex federal partners
description: On 17 June 2009 President Barack Obama signed a Presidential Memorandum granting certain benefits to the same-sex partners of federal employees, though it stopped short of full health coverage.

sources.label: PBS American Experience — Milestones in the American Gay Rights Movement

title: Matthew Shepard Act expands federal hate crime law
description: On 28 October 2009 Congress passed and President Barack Obama signed the Matthew Shepard Act, extending federal hate crime protections to offenses motivated by gender, sexual orientation, gender identity or disability. It was named for Matthew Shepard, who was tortured and murdered near Laramie, Wyoming in October 1998.

sources.label: PBS American Experience — Milestones in the American Gay Rights Movement

title: Federal court strikes down Proposition 8
description: On 4 August 2010 a federal judge in San Francisco ruled that gay and lesbian couples had a constitutional right to marry and that California's Proposition 8 was unconstitutional.

sources.label: PBS American Experience — Milestones in the American Gay Rights Movement

title: Senate votes to repeal Don't Ask, Don't Tell
description: On 18 December 2010 the U.S. Senate voted 65 to 31 to repeal Don't Ask, Don't Tell, clearing the way for gay and lesbian service members to serve openly in the military.

sources.label: PBS American Experience — Milestones in the American Gay Rights Movement

title: Obama administration stops defending DOMA
description: On 23 February 2011 President Barack Obama announced that his administration would no longer defend the Defense of Marriage Act in court, viewing its core provision as unconstitutional.

sources.label: PBS American Experience — Milestones in the American Gay Rights Movement

title: New York legalizes same-sex marriage
description: On 24 June 2011 New York passed the Marriage Equality Act, becoming the largest U.S. state up to that point to legalize same-sex marriage.

sources.label: PBS American Experience — Milestones in the American Gay Rights Movement

title: Supreme Court legalizes same-sex marriage nationwide in Obergefell v. Hodges
description: On 26 June 2015 the U.S. Supreme Court ruled 5 to 4 in Obergefell v. Hodges that same-sex marriage was legal in all fifty states, requiring every state to license and recognize such marriages.

sources.label: PBS American Experience — Milestones in the American Gay Rights Movement
